text: The surgical field shows no visible signs of inflammatory tissue or previous infection, indicating a clear and healthy operative area.
The surgeon observes that the areolar tissue is very light, facilitating the identification of the critical view of safety during the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the hepatic fossa after identifying the critical view, maintaining precision in separating the gallbladder from the liver bed.
After verifying the critical view, the surgeon places three clips on the cystic duct and three clips on the cystic artery, securing these structures for subsequent division.
The surgeon transects the cystic duct and cystic artery between the placed clips, completing their separation from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to remove the gallbladder from the hepatic fossa, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a thin areolar plane between the gallbladder and the liver, indicating a clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon performs careful dissection to prevent bleeding from the liver bed and avoid puncturing the gallbladder, which could lead to bile spillage.
The surgical bed is revisited, showing a hemostatic liver with clips securely in place on the cystic duct and artery.
The operation is complete, with the gallbladder successfully removed from the fossa and the surgical site appearing hemostatic.
